ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for chilli pakoda; A0EJX (fritters and battered fried vegetables) is the closest defensible category match.

allergens: gluten (gram flour may contain traces depending on processing)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of chilli pakoda consists of 3–4 pieces weighing approximately 120 g, commonly served as a street-food snack or appetizer alongside mint chutney or tamarind sauce.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Besan (gram flour) and fried snack composites; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for green chilli and frying-oil contribution
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for chilli pakoda as a composite dish. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for besan-based fried snacks (pakoda/bhajia category) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for green chilli (NDB 11670) and frying oil absorption. The input record values (energy 232 kcal, protein 6 g, fat 16 g, carbohydrate 18 g per 100 g) are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Cholesterol is 0 mg as the dish contains no animal-derived ingredients.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database entry for chickpea flour (besan) based foods
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chilli pakoda. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being gram flour (besan), which has a published GI of approximately 39–45 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (chickpea flour-based foods, GI range 32–45). The lower end of the besan GI range is applied here as the dish contains whole green chillies (high fibre, low GI) and no potato filling, which would otherwise raise the GI. This record assumes the standard green-chilli-only filling without potato stuffing. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 21.6) / 100 ≈ 9.7, using 120 g serving × 18 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 21.6 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 340 mg per 100 g; borderline high for a snack food
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit chilli pakoda to a small portion (60–80 g) and request reduced salt in the batter, as sodium at 340 mg/100 g approaches the threshold for high-sodium snacks; avoid pairing with high-sodium chutneys or dipping sauces.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low-to-moderate GI (~45) from gram flour batter; 18 g carbohydrate per 100 g; deep-frying increases caloric density
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should treat chilli pakoda as an occasional snack, limiting portions to 60–80 g to keep glycemic load below 8, and avoid pairing with sweet chutneys that add rapidly absorbed sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 16 g/100 g with saturated fat 3.2 g/100 g and trans fat 0.2 g/100 g from deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit chilli pakoda due to its high total fat content (16 g/100 g) and the presence of trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) from deep-frying; an air-fried or baked preparation would substantially reduce fat and trans fat exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); green chillies are nightshade vegetables that may trigger flares in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried chilli pakoda as the AGE load from deep-frying promotes systemic inflammation; an air-fried or baked preparation with turmeric and cumin would reduce AGE formation and provide anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chilli Pakoda (also called Mirchi Pakoda or Mirchi Bajji) is a popular Indian deep-fried snack made by coating large whole green chillies in a spiced gram flour (besan) batter and frying them until golden and crisp. The batter is typically seasoned with salt, turmeric, red chilli powder, cumin, and carom seeds (ajwain), giving the fritter a savoury, mildly spicy, and aromatic flavour. The outer shell is crunchy while the chilli inside softens during frying, creating a contrast of textures. The dish is entirely plant-based (vegan and halal) and contains no animal products, making it suitable for vegetarian and vegan diets. Nutritionally, chilli pakoda provides approximately 232 kcal per 100 g, with 16 g fat (predominantly from frying oil), 18 g carbohydrate (primarily from gram flour), and 6 g protein. Dietary fibre is notable at 3.2 g/100 g, contributed by both the gram flour and the green chilli. Sodium is 340 mg/100 g, which is borderline high. Vitamin C is present at 16 mg/100 g from the green chilli. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low) based on the gram flour base and the fibre contribution of whole green chillies. Clinically, the dish is a moderate concern for type 2 diabetes due to its carbohydrate profile and caloric density from deep-frying, a moderate concern for hypertension due to sodium content, and a moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to total fat and trans fat from deep-frying. It is widely served as a street food, tea-time snack, or appetizer across India and in Indian communities in Singapore, often accompanied by mint chutney or tamarind sauce.
common_user_queries: How many calories in mirchi bajji?
Is chilli pakoda suitable for diabetics?
What is chilli bajji made of?
What is the GI of besan fried snacks?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for chilli pakoda as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN besan-based fried snack data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for green chilli and frying-oil contribution. The input values are consistent with this composite estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for gram flour-based foods (GI 39–45). Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation, micronutrient, and moisture data for this composite deep-fried besan snack are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a single composite entry as of 2026. 2. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): the dish contains no animal-derived ingredients; cholesterol is naturally zero in a fully plant-based preparation, confirmed by the ingredient profile and consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for gram flour and green chilli. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.2 g/100 g, retained from the input record; this is plausible for deep-frying in partially hydrogenated or repeatedly heated oil. 4. The input record listed two default portions (250 g and 200 g); the serving size has been normalised to 120 g (3–4 pieces) as a more conservative and clinically appropriate standard serving. 5. The input ethnic_tags included 'Butter Chicken' and 'Curry' which are irrelevant to this dish and have been removed. 6. country_of_origin in the input was listed as Singapore; this has been corrected to reflect Indian origin with Singapore as the serving context. 7. GI revised to 45 (low) from the previous draft value of 55 (medium). The University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 report chickpea flour (besan) GI in the range 32–45; 45 is the upper defensible bound for a besan-dominant preparation without potato filling. gi_category corrected to 'low' (GI ≤ 55 per standard classification; GI 45 falls in the low category per the University of Sydney three-tier classification: low < 55, medium 55–69, high ≥ 70). Glycemic load per serving recalculated as (45 × 21.6) / 100 = 9.7.

Iteration 2: Added GI (55, analogous estimate), computed GL per serving (11.9), added all five mandatory Singaporean-cuisine language forms, corrected ethnic_tags, normalised serving to 120 g, corrected country_of_origin to India, populated health_context with all three required conditions, documented all -1 fields and confirmed-zero cholesterol in notes, verified aliases consistency.

Glycemic load per serving recalculated from 11.9 to 9.7. gi_source notes updated to reflect GI 45 rationale. type2_diabetes guidance updated to reflect revised GL. notes section updated with GI revision explanation.
